title: Object.hasOwn Check
description: Own properties vs inherited prototype chain.

questions.explanation: `shared` is on the prototype, not an own property. `"shared" in user` is true, but `Object.hasOwn` returns false for inherited keys.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `"key" in obj` and `Object.hasOwn(obj, "key")`?

options.text: `in` includes inherited keys; `hasOwn` checks only own properties

questions.explanation: The `in` operator walks the prototype chain. `Object.hasOwn` asks whether the property exists directly on the object.

questions.prompt: For a plain object `record = { id: 1 }`, what is `Object.hasOwn(record, "toString")`?
questions.explanation: `toString` is inherited from `Object.prototype`, not an own property of `record`.

questions.prompt: Why prefer `Object.hasOwn(obj, key)` over `obj.hasOwnProperty(key)` in library code?

options.text: Some objects may null out or override `hasOwnProperty`
`Object.hasOwn` is slower but required by syntax

questions.explanation: `Object.hasOwn` is the safe built-in that does not rely on the object possibly missing or overriding `hasOwnProperty`.
Assigning `config.theme = "light"` creates an own property shadowing the prototype. Reading `config.theme` returns `"light"`.
